speaker: interviewer
text: This is reel nine, interview with Henry Rosmarin. Henry, you were talking about the reunion with your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can imagine the joyful reunion that was. And oh, I got the guy's name-- Obersky-- the Polish innkeeper, Obersky, Yisrael Obersky-- what a wonderful guy. At any rate, from there on, we swore we would never separate. And again, the pain came. It was a joyful and tearful reunion-- tearful both because we were thankful to be alive, and tearful also because-- a bittersweet reunion. Two shipwrecks-- finally survived this horrible storm, being tossed around helplessly on the oceans of life and war through war-torn Europe. And I hate to be philosophical about it, but that's more or less how I like to describe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet, too happy, and yet bitter, because the question is where's Mom? Where's Dad? And I remember my brother asked me, what about Dad? What's happened? What happened to Dad? When he asked me, where's Dad, what's happened to Dad, it was almost like saying to me, I gave you something for safekeeping. What did you do with it? You see, my guilt, again, surfaced. Why didn't-- why couldn't I help my dad? Why couldn't I have helped him to survive? And-- and I had to know what I could have done other than what I did? But yet, I felt this horrible guilt when he says, where's Dad? What happened? What happened? And of course, I broke out crying again. The pain returned again, and I told him what happened. And he says, thank God-- oh, thank God you at least are alive. And I say, what about Mom? He says, no. They went straight to-- he told me he went to Auschwitz. And how he survived, he volunteered. That's another story which I won't get into.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he volunteered-- as they emptied the box cars, he saw with selection on the siding of rail siding, someone with the doctor's insignias. And they were selecting people-- left, right, left, right. So he jumped in front of this guy with a long coat with a doctor's insignia on his lapels and volunteers. He says, I volunteer-- called him by a rank name, whatever-- [GERMAN], whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after that, he would ask a pointed question. Have you been-- have you had malaria? Have you had jaundice? Have you had the smallpox? He says, no, no. And he was healthy looking-- as I said, a fair-- a fair complexion, blondish hair, and blue eyes. He says, no. He's healthy. He was healthy.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was segregated to the right. He had no idea what for. As it turned out, he-- I still have his stuff in my possession. And as it turned out, he volunteered for-- to be a human guinea pig on the Dr. Mengele-- that doctor was Dr. Mengele. And they needed-- they made experiments on him to experiment trying to find antidotes, antibiotics against malaria, because General Rommel was fighting a war in North Africa then, and malaria was one of the big sicknesses that he had to conquer. So-- so they were experimenting on human beings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how he survived. And we thought at first we will stay in Poland. And after a couple months or so, we keep saying, no, we've got to get out of Poland. It's getting anti-Semitics in, and there's nothing-- there's no future for us here. And I said, where will we go? They know us here. The mayor of town gave us a beautiful apartment from an ex-Nazi, furnished. He kicked him out. He gave us the keys, because he was a coal miner also. He knew our father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I said, OK, so we'll go back to school. So I enrolled in school. He did too. And another month went by. This is now July, August, and already, anti-Semites show their ugly faces. And there were some anti-Jewish slogan, anti-Semitic slogan. And he said, we gotta get-- I said, let's wait another month. And I told him the truth. I was hoping-- my only hope was then I knew my dad, he didn't make it. My mom didn't make it. Thank god for my brother. If Jadzia just would be alive, that would compensate at least for some. I just hope she's alive, because that meant a lot to me. When we parted, I don't know if I mentioned before, but I told her how much I love her and how someday-- we made sort of like a pact that we would return to this place again, whoever survives comes back to this place, to each other, in Siemianowice, in one of those places. We'll find each other. But here is already one month gone by, two and three. People are returning, and no sign of Jadzia. So I begged him. I said, let's stay for the high holidays at least. Rabbi Lieberman, by that time-- he survived too in hiding. And he organized the prayer services. So we stayed close to the high holidays. So he agreed to stay until after the holidays.

words.text: this place, to each other, in

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is now late August or maybe beginning of September. And every time someone would show up from camps, any returnees, I would ask, did you see-- women, most, of course. What camp were you in? And if they mentioned Auschwitz or Birkenau, I would ask, did you happen to run across so and so?

speaker: interviewee
text: And once in a while, I would get a lead. Someone would say, oh, wait a minute. Yeah, I remember her. She had typhoid, I believe, a month before liberation. I said, oh, gosh. She'll never make it. She didn't make it. So I agree to leave Poland. And by this time, it's already late August in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then one day, my cousin, who also survived-- and she lives here in Los Angeles, actually. She heard me always talking about Jadzia. And by the time I had another friend of mine-- girls we used to run around with-- she had a picture of her and herself together. And she gave me the picture. And I would show up, and I would go looking for her and say, did you ever see this girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I did have a picture. In the camps, I never had a picture of Jadzia, just a mental picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the camps, did you think about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot, yeah. Yeah. A lot-- in fact, in the camps, she was sort of-- like one would throw a life preserver on the ocean you hold onto. This was something I would hold onto, because I had very little hope to hold on to survive and see my mom and dad again. I said, one of the two, maybe both-- either Jadzia or my brother. Maybe one of them will be alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in fact, when I pray for the [INAUDIBLE], I kept saying to myself, I need extra strength, and I can say, maybe if I do right, I'll survive, and I'll maybe see her again. So it was a puppy love, yet a deep love, because the circumstances under which this relationship developed forced us to grow up all of a sudden from-- from-- There was no puppy. There was no adolescence in between, you see. So yes, I did have a good mental picture, but no actual pictures of her. So this, again, returning to August 1945, my cousin, who then-- she survived, and I thought, she can live with us. We had a big four room apartment, so she had her own place. And she went to shop, to the marketplace. And there was also a stop-- a tram would stop, would be the end station. And she was shopping at a marketplace. Some people got off the street car. And one of them approached my cousin Regina, and would tell her in Polish, excuse me, please, would you know of any Jewish people living here in this town? And my cousin Regina would say, yes, a few. But why are you asking me? And Jews in Poland would somehow recognize one another. There was something in your eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you-- if you didn't have a word like, say, [HEBREW], which means-- it's a Hebrew word. I don't know how it was adopted in-- in Ashkenazi. But one word, [HEBREW], and if-- and if you answered positively, then of course, you were one of the tribe. And this was almost like a password.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so she would ask her, why are you asking me if I know any Jewish people? So this girl who got off the street car, kind of skinny, short cut hair, undernourished-- she could hardly walk, weak-- says, well, because I thought you might be Jewish. She says, well, as a matter of fact, yes, I am Jewish. But why are you asking me? Who are you looking for? She says, I'm looking for Henry. She says, oh, my God. You must be Jadzia! And it was Jadzia. And Jadzia says to her, how do you know my name? And who are you? And how do you know my name? She says Henry is my cousin. He carries your picture around. He talks about you day and night. Oh, my god. I have to take you to him. So she comes running to the apartment. She says, Henry-- out of breath. She says, I got some news for you. She says, you better sit down. I don't want you to faint. I say, what is it? I figured maybe by some miracle, my mom, maybe my dad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it hit me-- oh, maybe Jadzia. So somebody is downstairs. I have somebody here for you. So I'm pushing her away, aside. I'm running downstairs. And I'll never forget the picture of poor little Jadzia stays there with her coat kind of faded, little brown, brownish, coat, not pressed, wrinkled, and short, cropped hair, kind of white-ish, the peaches and cream cheeks were gone. And the black pig tails, beautiful, curly pig tails were gone-- just little short, cropped hair. But the beautiful brown eyes were sticking out, dark eyes were like two black coals out of her eye socket. And without thinking-- we, incidentally, never had any privacy of hugging, even. So I hugged her. And for the first time, we kissed. That was our first kiss. And of course, we haven't parted since that day. And I'm happy to tell you that last year-- in fact, a few weeks ago, we celebrated-- last May, we had our golden wedding anniversary. And two weeks ago, we had our 51st anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the-- by the-- another-- another miracle happened. As you see, my story is full of miracles, because every survivor's life is full of miracles. So she did survive. She survived Birkenau-Auschwitz. She survived starvation. She survived death march. She survived Bergen-Belsen. And beautiful miracles happened to her. A doctor who had-- a French doctor who had a girl about her age sort of adopted her in camp and gave her extra rations and tried to help her-- a Jewish doctor, an inmate-- hoping that, he kept telling her, maybe the one above will send a guardian angel to save my own daughter too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so she survived. And we've been married 51 years now and raised a family. And we have two sons. We came to this country in 1948. We spent some time-- [INAUDIBLE] sent refugees after the war to various cities. My brother wound up in Kentucky, of all places. I joined him later, and then we lived in Indiana. And he, of course, married, raised a family too. He has two wonderful children and grandchildren. Unfortunately, he didn't live to see his grandchildren. He got killed in an automobile accident on an Indiana highway almost 20 years ago, which was a very ironic ending for a guy who-- who-- who got out alive out of the oven, so to speak, and to get killed standing still on a traffic light, sideswiped by a tractor trailer. Life is full of ironies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So unfortunately, he didn't get to see his grandchildren. But he lived a wonderful legacy. Let's go back a second. After you saw Jadzia again in this miraculous reunion--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did-- what did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, of course, we realized our dream about leaving Poland. We decided we're going to leave Poland. Poland became communist then. They had some kind of a referendum, phony kind of elections. And we heard that-- of course, I went back to school. And I wanted to pursue my music career. I wanted to learn music. And my brother went to a higher learning school, also. He wanted to go to university. So one night, we went to the German-Polish border and paid a smuggler a little money to take us across to Germany to Czechoslovakia. There's a corner. Czechoslovakia, Germany, and Poland meet in that corner, a little town called Cieszyn. And we want across that. And he did smuggle [NON-ENGLISH], in those days, what is now Israel, where Palestine organizations were helping Jewish refugees, survivors, get out of Poland. And Bratislava was the point of, more of less, the assembly point. So they took us to Bratislava. From there, they transported us to Vienna. And we spent the next two years in Austria until 1948, and the DP came first, and then on our own private homes. And then we applied for visa to enter to the United States. My brother, meantime, got accepted to the University of Vienna. And he finished three semesters. And he wanted to finish school. But the temptation of, when his time came, when the visa came through to come to the United States, he opted to go to the United States instead, hoping he could go back to school here, which he did part time. But then a family came among, children. And it was hard to pursue school. But that's how we wound up-- from Vienna, we came to the United States in--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and Jadzia were married in 1947?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were married in 1947 in Austria. And we came to United States in 1948, almost to the day of our first wedding anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came-- the technical part was, of course, you had to have an affidavit. This is-- this is not an illegal immigration. This is the affidavit, a sponsor. And of course, the Joint Distribution, the Jewish organization, helped-- helped with affidavits for survivors who had no families, who had no next of kin. And I had no one in the United States. So I had to depend on private sponsors. My brother was very clever. He met someone at the university, a professor who introduced him to an American philanthropist. This lady was well to do. And she was supposed to give 10 affidavits to students, Jewish students, to immigrate to the United States. And a professor recommended my brother as one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so he had an interview with her at the chancellor's office. And she told him that she would sponsor him. And he thanked her for it. But he says he couldn't go. He has a brother. He wants to go with his brother. She says, is he a student too? He says, yeah, but he goes to music school. And she says, how old is he? And he told her it's a younger brother. She says, OK. Tomorrow I'll take care of his papers. So she came to us and brought some papers with my name on it. And then he told her, you know, I forgot to tell you, but he also is engaged. He's going to be married pretty soon. She says, well, you have a lot of guts, young man. You bring relatives from all over. He says, well, he can't go without her. And I won't go without him. So she signed another affidavit for us. She said, anybody else? And her name-- I forgot what her name was. But I remember my affidavit, and I still have a copy of it someplace. And I remember reading it later. Her name was Andre Bulova, which meant nothing to me until I came to the United States. We landed in Boston the 19th of November 1948. It was 50 years just recently.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remembered for the first time in my life seeing lit up billboards. And we got on this train. We passed the different stations. I see billboards. And then we came to Times Square of New York. We had to change trains. I went to Indianapolis, Indiana. We tried change trains in New York. And I kept looking at this big billboard in Times Square. And it said, Bulova Watches. And it turned out to be my sponsor was the president of, Andre Bulova of Bulova Watches. That's who signed my papers. So I said to myself, gee, I must have a famous sponsor here. I could have gone for a job. I didn't, of course. But I did write him a letter later, in my broken English, thanking him for-- for-- for what he did. So that was my trip to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come across in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In boats, what we called troop carriers, which was a wonderful voyage. For one thing, the only downside of it was sea sickness, because this was November, and seas were kind of choppy. But the beautiful part about it that food was-- this was a post-war year, but we never had enough of it. Even in Austria, food was rationed. And here, all of a sudden, I came on an American boat. It was a troop carrier. You would line up in the cafeteria, and it was a tray three times a day. And they give you-- I remember, for breakfast, I didn't know what it was. But it had pieces of meat in it on toast. It was like chopped beef-- army food-- and oranges and apples and bananas. Oh, gosh.

speaker: interviewer
text: I lived on oranges and apples. I didn't see oranges and apples in years, you know? And I lived on that. And unfortunately, bad weather came. I had to get rid of it go over the banister into the ocean. But the food was great. And they treated us well. And we had shows on board. And I had my little harmonica with me, and I played that too. And we were so happy, not a penny-- didn't have anything, didn't know the language, but happy to come to-- to-- to a country which-- and I must say, I can't say enough about the hospitality and the greatness of this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: They welcomed us with open arms. GIs returning after the war, looking for jobs, and here we are, looking for jobs also and for places to live. And somehow, we didn't hear the anti-Semitic slogans that we heard when we left Poland. We were in the home of the free, finally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, I wanted to ask you, because we're coming towards the end of this, a couple of questions that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --sort of reflective questions. Is there anything about your camp experience that you didn't talk about that you would like to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know we've talked a lot, but how we can one-- what comes to mind is sad, acts of-- acts of-- inhumane acts of murderous acts, and some humane acts too. I witnessed someone having his head split open to acts of courage, like helping the stragglers at the end who couldn't march anymore on the death march. And one or two guys would carry him. And-- and I didn't want to come to that point. That's why I wanted to make a run for it, unless-- death march-- someone should carry me, because as you carried and helped the straggling, the dying one, like on the ocean, they would pull you down with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I have seen acts of courage like that. I have seen acts of courage of believing-- religious-- religious acts of courage, such as-- I remember in this one camp-- in Dyhernfurth, as a matter of fact, at night, I would wake up, and the guy below me, his bunk was empty. And I looked down. He must have went to the toilet outside, outside toilet. But time goes by, he's not back-- half an hour. And I said, where could he be? And I began to get worried. Surely, he didn't grab the wire, because sometimes, they would go out and grab the wire and commit suicide. So I would look around, get up. And I finally found him at the end of the-- of the barrack-- of the room, between two other berths. And I see this figure-- what am I trying to say-- rocking back and forth in a standing position. He was paying at night. He got up-- somehow, he got a little prayer book. I don't know how he smuggled it in-- a small, little prayer book. And he was flicking pages and praying. And I certainly thought that was a courageous act, because if they would catch him praying or his prayer book, they would kill him. And when they were distributing food, in the soup, sometimes, you would find a piece of meat swimming, which would be a piece of bacon, perhaps. He would find one. He would shove it aside. A starving inmate would push it aside. He's not going to touch it. And he wouldn't let another Jewish guy touch it. But he would talk to, say, a Russian inmate or Ukranian or what have you, and would say, would you like this soup? I will trade you for two potatoes. So he would take two potatoes or three. And he would trade off the soup, and he would eat the potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It would take a lot of courage to, under those circumstances, to be a believer and to practice it. And so you see, I see both sides of the coin. Numerous acts like this I could describe. It would take volumes of tapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about your own Jewishness when you were going through that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first-- I have to be honest. If I'm not honest, then this whole thing is not worth doing. I really had my doubts about a higher being, you know? And in the beginning, even before the war broke out, when I was denied access to-- I wanted to join the Boy Scouts in Siemianowice. And it was like 1938, a year before the war broke out. And I was denied because I was Jewish. And I wanted to wear the short pants uniform that I saw Hitler [GERMAN]. They were not supposed to be in the open, but they were wearing lederhosen, with the swastikas in the lapels.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I thought that was kind of cool, to use a present term. And I wanted to join that way. So all of a sudden, I couldn't do that. But I really got hurt about Boy Scouts. And I remember thinking to myself, why was I born a Jew? Why wasn't I born an equal, like the rest of my friends? So I really felt inferior in a way. And I felt I'm being deprived of something because I was born a Jew. And as far as believing, I-- I obeyed my-- my-- my parents' wishes, but I had doubts. And then as war came along, and I've seen the killing, I had my own questions, my own doubts about why is the one above allowing this? I couldn't understand what's happening all around, all this madness? Children are being slaughtered. Have you ever answered that question for yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Have I answered the question to myself? Of course, as you-- as you get older-- it's still hard to-- to-- to reconcile when you see children being slaughtered. I saw mothers throwing childre-- I was marching towards the station from the first assembly place. And I heard a thump. And a lady was carrying something. And she said to the peasants who were standing, leaning over the fence. She said, [NON-ENGLISH]. She threw a bundle, and I heard a thump falling. And we were marching further.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she just looking back, and she threw her baby over the fence. So it took an act of courage, and the toughness it took for a mother to throw-- what about my own mom, who kept saying, go, save yourself? And as I became a father myself, I keep thinking how courageous my mother was, and my dad, trying to protect us from all this madness, and how much courage it takes for a mother to part with her son, with her child who she loves so much, and say, go, you belong here now, here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I kept clinging to her. And she pushed me away, as if she didn't want me. So as I became a parent, I kept-- so you see, your outlook, the way you see things as a young kid, and if you're in the middle of this, as I described in the beginning of my testimony about being in the center of the eye of the storm, it's hard sometimes, to see any reason. But now, I can see many courageous acts and what it took for a mother or a father to-- what they had to suffer to see separation of families.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to change cassettes now, Henry.
